criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the core Many-for-Many (MfM) unified training framework, including the adapter design, transformer architecture, and training strategy.
The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand how different task inputs are categorized into 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D conditions and unified via the adapter.

The plan includes a lightweight adapter that converts 3D pixel-space conditional inputs Y (T×H×W×C) to latent-space feature maps y (t×h×w×c) using convolution layers and downsampling blocks, with t=T/4, h=H/8, w=W/8 matching the VAE compression ratios.
The adapter concatenates pixel data, depth maps, and mask conditions along the channel dimension into a single 3D conditional tensor before processing.
The adapter output is added to the latent space features from the video VAE encoder (additive integration), enabling conditioning without replacing the VAE latent.
Task name strings (e.g., 'text-to-video', 'image-to-video') are appended to text prompts to disambiguate tasks that share similar mask inputs.
For the T2V task, pixel, depth map, and mask inputs are all set to zero so generation is driven solely by the text prompt.
For the I2V task, only the conditions of the first frame (pixel, depth, mask) are provided as the conditional input.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the 3D full attention transformer, 3D RoPE, and Q-K Normalization components.

The DiT transformer block includes three sub-modules: a self-attention module for intra-sequence relationships, a cross-attention layer for text embeddings, and an AdaLN (Adaptive Layer Normalization) for embedding timestep and motion score.

3D RoPE is implemented by applying 1D RoPE independently to temporal (t), height (h), and width (w) dimensions, then concatenating the encodings. The temporal dimension occupies 2/8 of hidden state channels, and each spatial dimension (h, w) occupies 3/8.
The plan includes Q-K Normalization using RMSNorm applied to query and key vectors before computing attention scores to stabilize training of large-scale DiT models.
RMSNorm-based Query-Key Normalization (QK-Norm) is implemented and applied to the attention queries and keys in each transformer block.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the Rectified Flow training objective, multi-task joint learning strategy, and progressive resolution training pipeline.
The plan includes the Rectified Flow (Flow Matching) training procedure including linear interpolation of noisy inputs and velocity prediction.
The model input X_t is computed as linear interpolation X_t = (1-t)*X_0 + t*epsilon, where t is sampled uniformly from [0, 1] and epsilon is Gaussian noise.

The training objective L = E[|mu_theta(t, X_t, ms, c, Y) - V_t|^2] is implemented, where V_t = epsilon - X_0 is the ground-truth velocity and the model predicts this velocity given timestep t, noisy input X_t, motion score ms, text prompt c, and 3D conditional input Y.
Logit-Normal Sampling is used for timestep t during training, following SD3.

The plan includes the joint image-video learning strategy with progressively decreasing image-to-video ratio during training, starting from pure image data and decreasing to a 0.1 image-to-video ratio.
The image-to-video ratio in training data is progressively decreased from an initial phase of pure image-text pairs down to a ratio of 0.1 as training progresses.
At each training step, a video is assigned a set of qualified tasks and one task is randomly selected to construct the conditional input, forming the multi-task training loop.
The sampling probability of T2V, I2V, and T2I tasks is set to 3× higher than other tasks during multi-task training.
10% of text prompts are randomly replaced with null-text for T2V/T2I tasks (for classifier-free guidance), and 10% chance of zeroing 3D conditional inputs for non-T2V/T2I tasks.
The progressive resolution training pipeline is implemented with four stages: 128px (49×128×224), 360px (89×352×640), 720px (97×720×1280), and multi-resolution with NaViT-style packing.

The 8B model training recipe is implemented according to Table 2: 128px stage uses lr=1e-4, bs=16/GPU for 170k iters; 360px stage uses lr=8e-5, bs=2 for 100k iters; 720px stage uses lr=5e-5 for 50k iters; multi-res stage uses lr=5e-5 for 40k iters.
FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallelism) and Sequence Parallelism (SP) are used to enable efficient and scalable training.
The 8B model hyperparameters are implemented: 40 transformer layers, 48 attention heads, head dimension 64, FFN dimension 12288, cross-attention dimension (3072, 2048).
The 2B model hyperparameters are implemented: 28 transformer layers, 28 attention heads, head dimension 64, FFN dimension 7168, cross-attention dimension (1792, 2048).
The plan includes using CogVideoX video VAE (with 8× spatial and 4× temporal compression ratios) as the encoder/decoder.
The plan includes using an LLM text encoder (T5-based, following Raffel et al. 2020) to encode text prompts.
RAFT optical flow is used to compute motion scores (mean optical flow of video clips) that are integrated into MfM training via AdaLN.
Depth maps are predicted on-the-fly using a lightweight depth estimation model (Depth Anything V2) and concatenated as the depth channel of the 3D conditional input.

The multi-task data augmentation pipeline is implemented with all 16 task-specific conditioning methods as defined in Appendix A.3: T2V (original captions), I2V (first frame + caption), VEXT (first 8 frames), VINP (random interior masks covering 1/9-1/4 of pixels), VOUTP (boundary masks covering 1/8-1/4 of width/height), VCOLOR (grayscale conversion), FLF2V (first+last frame conditioning), FLC2V (first 8 + last 8 frames), VSR (random 2×-6× downsampling), VEDIT (style instruction prompts), and image-level variants.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand training data preparation, evaluation benchmarks, and inference parameters.

The MfM-benchmark is constructed from 480 high-quality videos from Pexels with 30 samples per task across 16 tasks, filtered for sharpness (CV2.Laplacian >= 200) and motion (RAFT motion score > 5), standardized to 97 frames.
The inference configuration is set to 30 diffusion steps with classifier-free guidance scale of 9.0 for all experiments.
The agent has read Section 4.2 and Tables 3-4 to understand the T2V and I2V evaluation results on VBench.

The plan includes running T2V evaluation on VBench-T2V benchmark with 11 metrics: motion smoothness, dynamic degree, aesthetic quality, imaging quality, object class accuracy, multiple objects, spatial relationship, scene consistency, appearance style, temporal style, and overall consistency.
T2V generation code is implemented with the full model (adapter + DiT with 3D full attention + 3D RoPE + QK-Norm) for evaluation.
The T2V evaluation code has been executed successfully on VBench-T2V benchmark without errors.

The T2V evaluation results match Table 3: MfM achieves the best average rank (2.86), ranking first in appearance style and overall consistency, and second in dynamic degree, aesthetic quality, multiple object generation, and spatial relationship.

The plan includes running I2V evaluation on VBench-I2V benchmark with 7 metrics: image subject consistency, image background consistency, motion smoothness, dynamic degree, aesthetic quality, imaging quality, and average rank.
I2V generation code is implemented correctly, using the first frame pixel, depth map, and mask as conditioning inputs.
The I2V evaluation code has been executed successfully on VBench-I2V benchmark without errors.

The I2V evaluation results match Table 4: MfM achieves the best average rank (3.33), excelling in imaging quality (0.718) and achieving balanced performance across static consistency and dynamic generation.
The agent has read Section 4.3 and Table 5 to understand the multi-task video manipulation experiments and baselines.

The plan includes implementing four video manipulation tasks (VINP, VOUTP, FLF2V, VCOLOR) with their respective baselines: VINP vs VACE and ProPainter, VOUTP vs VACE/FYC/M3DDM, FLF2V vs Wanx/Hunyuan, VCOLOR vs ColorMNet/TCVC.
Video inpainting (VINP) code is implemented: random interior masks covering 1/9 to 1/4 of pixels are used as conditional masks, with pixel and depth map zero-padded for masked regions.
Video outpainting (VOUTP) code is implemented: boundary masks covering 1/8 to 1/4 of the total width/height are used as conditional masks.
Video colorization (VCOLOR) code is implemented: grayscale-converted videos are used as pixel conditions with original captions.
First-last-frame-to-video (FLF2V) code is implemented: first and last frames are used as conditioning inputs to generate the intermediate 95 frames.

The multi-task video manipulation evaluation code has been executed successfully on the MfM-Benchmark with reference-based metrics (FID, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) and perceptual metrics (aesthetic, imaging, motion, consistency, temporal).
VINP results match Table 5: MfM achieves FID=51.03, PSNR=21.31, SSIM=0.830, LPIPS=0.112, outperforming VACE (FID=67.51) and ProPainter (FID=119.93) on FID.
VOUTP results match Table 5: MfM achieves FID=44.15, outperforming VACE (FID=54.34), FYC (FID=94.69), and M3DDM (FID=174.61).
FLF2V results match Table 5: MfM achieves FID=31.98, PSNR=19.95, SSIM=0.583, outperforming Wanx (FID=38.24) and Hunyuan (FID=118.18) in FID.
The agent has read Section 4.4 and Table 6 to understand the ablation study on multi-task training versus single-task training.
The plan includes the ablation study comparing: pure T2V, T2V+single auxiliary task (VINP, VCOLOR, VSR, VOUTP, FLF2V, FLC2V, VEXT, I2V), MfM with depth, and MfM without depth.
Code is implemented for ablation experiments: models trained from a T2V baseline with single auxiliary tasks (VINP, VCOLOR, VSR, VOUTP, FLF2V, FLC2V, VEXT, I2V) for the same number of iterations.
Ablation experiments (Table 6) have been executed successfully, evaluating each T2V + single auxiliary task variant on VBench-T2V without errors.

Ablation results match Table 6: all single-task variants improve Scene metric over the T2V baseline; MfM with depth consistently outperforms T2V baseline across all metrics; removing depth degrades Dynamics by ~10%, Imaging Quality by ~4%, and Scene Consistency by ~12%.
The agent has read Table 13 (Appendix A.14) to understand the ablation study on Q-K Normalization and 3D RoPE contributions.
Ablation experiments for Q-K Norm and 3D RoPE are implemented by fine-tuning a 2B checkpoint for 10K training steps under three settings: full model, removing Q-K Norm, and removing 3D RoPE.

Ablation results match Table 13: removing Q-K Norm causes model collapse (training divergence) after ~3K steps; removing 3D RoPE does not cause divergence but causes severe degradation, especially on Object, Multiple Objects, Spatial, and Scene metrics (near-zero values).
The agent has read Table 10 (Appendix A.11) to understand the sampling probability ablation study.
Sampling probability ablation experiments (1×, 2×, 3×, 4× for basic tasks) are implemented and executed on VBench-T2V.

Sampling probability ablation results match Table 10: the 3× sampling strategy achieves the strongest overall performance, while 1× or 2× underperforms due to insufficient T2V updates, and 4× degrades due to weakened regularization from editing tasks.
The agent has read Table 11 (Appendix A.12) to understand the MovieGen benchmark evaluation.
The MovieGen Benchmark evaluation code has been executed successfully for T2V generation using VBench metrics (aesthetic, imaging, dynamic, temporal style, consistency).

MovieGen benchmark results match Table 11: MfM (8B) achieves best average rank (2.2), best performance on Dynamic (0.7188), and competitive performance on imaging quality and consistency compared to larger models like Wanx (14B) and Hunyuan (13B).
The agent has read Table 12 (Appendix A.13) to understand the task interaction ablation study.
Task interaction experiments are planned with combinations of T2V, VINP, FLF2V, VCOLOR to study pairwise and joint task interactions.
Task interaction ablation experiments (T2V only, T2V+single-task pairs, T2V+two-task combos, T2V+all four tasks) are implemented and executed on VBench-T2V.

Task interaction results match Table 12: T2V+FLF2V+VCOLOR+VINP achieves the best Dynamic (0.8750) and Consistency (0.2553); T2V+VINP+FLF2V achieves strong Motion (0.9925), Aesthetic (0.5947), and Object (0.9090).
The agent has read Table 14 (Appendix A.15) to understand the model adaptation ablation to new tasks.

Model adaptation experiment is implemented: starting from T2V model, Model 1 (10K T2V-only) and Model 2 (10K multi-task excluding VINP) are both fine-tuned on VINP and compared on FVD, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS metrics.

Adaptation results match Table 14: Model 2 (multi-task pretraining) outperforms Model 1 (T2V-only) in FVD (740.026 vs 758.894), PSNR (29.113 vs 28.740), SSIM (0.709 vs 0.699), and LPIPS (0.286 vs 0.296), showing multi-task pretraining improves adaptability.

The data curation pipeline is implemented with four stages: video segmentation (PySceneDetect + DINOv2 inter-frame similarity), video quality filtering (FPS/resolution/aesthetic/OCR/watermark/motion), semantic content filtering (VideoLLaMA3), and video captioning (Tarsier2 with short global + long detailed captions).

The user study for I2V generation is conducted with 10 test cases and 10 participants asked to rank top-3 videos, and the results match Table 9: MfM achieves 64.29% Top-1 rate and 85.71% Top-3 rate, outperforming all competitors including Wanx-I2V (20.00% Top-1) and Hunyuan-I2V (1.43% Top-1).
